# Questions in Livestream 189

Live Stream #189: The Thirteenthest of Fridays

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=1127" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:47</a> | What software do you use for guitar composition? | He said he uses Reaper as his DAW and MuseScore for composition and playback work. | He separates recording and composition tools: Reaper is his digital audio workstation, while MuseScore is where he likes to compose and hear playback. He framed those as his two preferred tools rather than giving a broader gear list. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=1195" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:55</a> | What did you think of the Billy Carson vs. Wes Huff debate? | He described it as badly one-sided, with Huff clearly better prepared and Carson's efforts to suppress clips only making the loss look worse. | He said he had seen part of the debate and characterized it as a beatdown rather than a real debate. In his view, Carson was not prepared for Huff's responses, and the later legal or takedown posture made the situation look worse because Carson had agreed to the debate format. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=1743" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">29:03</a> | Will David ever write a short book that is not heavy? | He joked about the wording, then said their novel had gone to beta readers and should be a manageable paperback. | He took the question lightly, then said the novel had just gone out to beta readers. He described it as a shorter, lighter project than his heavy academic books, estimating a manageable paperback length rather than another massive reference volume. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=2017" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:37</a> | Will anyone adapt the fiction book into a movie? | He said it is possible, but they do not have an agent, so the chances are lower. | He said movie adaptation can happen when producers read a novel and decide it would work as a film, but nothing like that was in motion. Since they do not have an agent, he treated the prospect as possible but not something to expect. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=2077" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:37</a> | Who is responsible for paying taxes on revenue from your book sales? | He said he is responsible, because royalties and book revenue are earned income. | He explained that revenue from book sales comes into his pocket, so he pays taxes on it. He compared it to royalties from his Ark of the Covenant book and treated it as ordinary taxable earned income. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=2141" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:41</a> | Is the extent of Egyptian imperial evidence in Canaan really only two garrisons and two administrative complexes? | He said no; those were the major sites, but there were also smaller outposts and garrisons. | He said the two garrisons and two administrative complexes were the big sites, not the whole picture; there were also smaller outposts, perhaps eight to ten garrison towns in total. He stressed that Egypt's Canaanite system was a hegemony, not an empire of direct daily control: the sites observed conditions, collected tribute, and served royal campaigns, while real coercion came through Egyptian military expeditions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=2447" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:47</a> | Would some Canaanite areas welcome and work with Egypt for defense? | He said some would, but Egyptian protection was unreliable; Egypt mainly cared that tribute kept flowing. | He allowed that some local rulers would work with Egypt, but said Egypt was not a dependable defender of Canaanite allies. Local powers were expected to handle their own defense; Egypt mainly intervened when tribute stopped, and rival local rulers might join Egypt against a rebel because it served their own interests. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=2581" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:01</a> | What were the perks of being pharaoh versus vizier versus nomarch, and which would you rather be? | He said pharaoh was the best position, with taxation, gifts, military power, and the top place in a corrupt patronage hierarchy. | He treated pharaoh as the best job because pharaoh could tax, receive gifts, command armies, and sit at the top of the system. Viziers and nomarchs could also become wealthy through administration and patronage, but they remained beneath pharaoh in a hierarchy where power and wealth flowed downward from the throne. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=2855" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:35</a> | How does the power of pharaoh compare to the power of modern Egyptian presidents? | He said both systems are military dictatorships in some respects, though pharaohs and modern presidents are constrained in different ways. | He compared them as rulers in military-dictatorship systems but said the constraints differ. Pharaoh could wage war against neighbors and was bound by tradition and elite expectations, while a modern Egyptian president has modern state tools and more influence over economic policy but faces different international and institutional limits. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=2986" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:46</a> | What is the "birds and bees" story? | He declined to explain it on the channel and told the viewer to ask parents or someone else. | He treated the question as outside the channel's scope and joked that this was not the place for that kind of education. The answer did not supply an explanation; it redirected the viewer elsewhere. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=3032" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:32</a> | What historical inaccuracies are in Tobit? | He said he had not read Tobit closely enough for that, but its theological problems, such as fish parts driving out demons and almsgiving saving from death, were enough to trouble him. | He declined to give a historical-inaccuracy list because he had not read Tobit thoroughly enough for that kind of claim. Instead, he said his objections were theological, especially the way fish parts function against demons and the way the book speaks about almsgiving, which he regarded as sufficient reason to view it as problematic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=3292" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:52</a> | How should Exodus 20:25-26 be explained? | He said the passage restricts earlier worship practices by requiring uncut stones and no stairs or ramp up to the altar. | He explained the command as part of a broader narrowing of acceptable worship after Sinai. Practices that had been used earlier, such as shaped altar stones, ramps, standing stones, and high-place worship, became restricted as Israel's worship was increasingly centralized and regulated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=3596" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:56</a> | What do the numbers 7, 10, 12, and 40 mean in the ancient Near East? | He said 7 marks holiness or the divine, 12 marks completeness, 40 marks an indefinite long period, and 10 has no fixed standalone meaning. | He warned against treating every number as a fixed code. He connected seven with holiness, twelve with perfection or completeness, and forty with an indefinite long period, while saying ten does not carry a specific meaning on its own except in special contexts such as defective-number patterns. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=3793" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:03:13</a> | Was Ramesses I a general and/or vizier? | He said yes: Ramesses I rose from charioteer to general, then vizier of Lower Egypt, and finally pharaoh. | He described Ramesses I as starting as a charioteer and becoming a notable general, known by his men as "the Ramesses." He was later promoted to vizier of Lower Egypt under Horemheb and then became the first pharaoh of the Nineteenth Dynasty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=3956" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:56</a> | Can we guess who Moses's adopted mother was? | He said probably not; she was likely a daughter of Ramesses I, but her name is not preserved. | He said the likely setting would make Moses's adopted mother a daughter of Ramesses I, but the relevant daughters are not well recorded by name. Because the names are missing, he said the data probably will not let us identify her. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=4011" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:51</a> | Who conquered and enslaved the Israelites in Avaris? | He identified Ahmose I as the king and Ahmose son of Abana as one of the generals involved. | He identified Ahmose I, the last king of the Seventeenth Dynasty and first of the Eighteenth, as the king who conquered Avaris around 1530 BC. He also named Ahmose son of Abana as at least one of the generals involved, noting that Ahmose was an extremely common name for men and women in that period. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=4073" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:53</a> | Why do we not hear more about the ancient Hittite and Amorite empires if they were so great? | He said the Amorites were a confederacy rather than an empire and left limited records, while the Hittites were great but received less popular attention than Egypt. | He said the Amorites were not really an empire but a loose confederacy or mutual-defense network, and the limited written record makes them harder to reconstruct. The Hittites did have a major empire, but fewer specialists, less visually appealing art and architecture, and less museum-driven popularization have kept them from receiving the public attention Egypt gets. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=4298" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:38</a> | Why is Tobias burning fish parts in Tobit not the same thing as Moses putting lamb's blood on Israelite doorways? | He distinguished the two by saying Tobit treats power as residing in the fish parts, while Passover blood functions as an act of faith and obedience to God. | He said the Passover blood did not have independent magical power; it was a ritual sign of faith that God would recognize. By contrast, he read Tobit as locating the effective power in the fish heart, liver, or gallbladder itself, which is why he treated the two examples as fundamentally different. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=4472" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:32</a> | If Ahmose was such a popular name, why did it take so long to get an Ahmose II? | He said Amenhotep became the more popular royal name, though Ahmose remained common among non-royal people. | He said Ahmose remained popular in civil documents and among princes and commoners into the middle of the Eighteenth Dynasty. It stopped being a common royal throne name after Ahmose I because Amenhotep became the more popular royal naming pattern. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=4738" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:58</a> | If Abraham lived during Hammurabi's time, which pharaoh would he have spoken with? | He said we do not know; Dynasty 13 had many short-reigning rulers, though Neferhotep I is one possibility. | He explained that Abraham's proposed period would fall in Dynasty 13, when kings changed frequently and many rulers are possible. Neferhotep I overlapped with Hammurabi and reigned longer than many others, but he said only a lucky textual discovery could identify the specific pharaoh. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=4843" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:43</a> | What clothes and shoes do you recommend for Egypt? | He recommended modest, heat-appropriate clothing, especially stricter coverage for women, and comfortable footwear such as sneakers. | He advised dressing for heat while still respecting Egyptian modesty expectations, with women especially avoiding revealing or tight clothing such as yoga pants. For footwear, he favored practical comfort, since travelers will be walking on rough surfaces and in hot conditions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=5692" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:52</a> | What do you think of John Walton's ideas about Genesis and animals surviving a world full of water? | He said the exact Walton idea was unclear, but he reads Genesis 1-11 as setting up sacred-space and ritual categories rather than concordist science. | He was cautious because he was not sure exactly which Walton claim the question had in mind. He said his own reading of Genesis 1-11 is not a concordist scientific reading; he sees those chapters as establishing sacred-space, ritual, and theological categories, and he would object if Walton were simply reshaping the text to fit modern science. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=6018" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:18</a> | What series would you recommend on evidence for the resurrection? | He did not know a specific series to recommend, but favored a maximal-facts approach over minimal-facts or presuppositional approaches. | He said resurrection apologetics was not his strongest area and did not name a series. Methodologically, he preferred a maximal-facts approach because it brings in more of the available evidence, while he was less favorable toward minimal-facts or purely presuppositional presentations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=6116" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:56</a> | What biblical scholars would you recommend checking out? | He named scholars such as Brent Strawn and Lydia McGrew, while warning that no scholar should be followed uncritically on everything. | He said there are many useful scholars and that recommendations should be topic-specific. He gave examples such as Brent Strawn and Lydia McGrew, but emphasized that even scholars he generally likes, such as John Walton, should not be treated as reliable on every subject. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=6647" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:47</a> | Why are there two images on the Kuntillet Ajrud inscription, with one figure interpreted as male in one and female in another? | He said the inscription is controversial, the drawings are not clearly tied to the text, and he does not see the middle figure as female. | He said the relationship between the drawings and inscription is unclear, so one should be cautious about tying the figures directly to the text. He rejected the idea that the middle figure is female, suggested the seated figure might be female, and said the extra images remain difficult to explain with confidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=6930" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:30</a> | Why would a science channel move from evidence for round earth and evolution into arguing that all religions are fake? | He said some channels extrapolate from one subject into another, but he did not think that was a good use of a science channel. | He treated the move as an example of people extending one kind of argument into a different domain. He did not offer a detailed refutation, but said the channel was free to do that as long as it was not pushed in his face, while calling it a poor use of a science channel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=7002" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:42</a> | How do you think the soul gets made, especially if we are born separated from God? | He said there are many views, and he leans toward an emergent view while admitting the data are not decisive. | He listed several views: God specially creates the soul at conception, the parents' souls combine in a traducian-type view, matter has spirit in a panpsychic view, the soul emerges from developing neural networks, or the soul begins at quickening or first breath. He said the topic is metaphysically complex and no view is nailed down with certainty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=7449" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:09</a> | How can someone debunk the Josian authorship theory for the Old Testament? | He said local Egyptian knowledge in the Torah is hard to explain under a late Josian authorship theory. | He pointed to Egyptian toponyms, archaic Egyptian loanwords, private mortuary practices, and details around Joseph as vizier as features that fit better with knowledge of Egypt than with a late divided-monarchy setting. He treated those data points as difficult for the theory that Josiah's scribes authored the Torah or Old Testament material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=7629" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:07:09</a> | When you mentioned Teru of Kesha's son dying, were you referring to a specific Mari-letter figure? | He said he would need to look it up; he remembered the point from a Mari letter but could not identify the exact letter live. | He said the question was too specific for the live setting because he would need to check the Mari letters. He remembered reading the reference, but could not confirm whether the named person in the question was the specific one without looking it up. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=7714" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:08:34</a> | How should someone explain the Incarnation to first and second graders? | He recommended teaching the basic "what" from Scripture, not trying to explain the full theological "how." | He said the hypostatic union is hard even for adults and theologians, so young children should be taught the basic facts: Jesus was fully God and fully man, and Scripture teaches it. He advised saving detailed church-council and theological explanation for when they are older. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=8129" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:29</a> | Why do kids have to teach adults biblical basics? | He said biblical literacy among adults is very poor because many have forgotten or never learned the basics, leaving children sometimes better prepared. | He said children can sometimes grasp basic teaching more simply, while many adults either were never taught or forgot what they once learned. He described the problem as biblical illiteracy and compared it to generational failure, where parents do not pass on the material and the next generation has to recover it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=8405" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:05</a> | In Judges 10:4, are the numbers symbolic, a scribal gloss, or a retrospective aside? | He said the thirty sons, donkeys, and cities are not just symbolic; they expose Jair's decadence and corruption. | He read Jair as one of the bad judges, with many wives or concubines and sons acting like little kings over cities. The repeated thirtys point to status, nepotism, and a corrupt mini-empire rather than a positive legacy or mere symbolic number. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=8945" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:05</a> | How can Protestants hold the Nicene Creed if ecumenical councils are fallible? | He said sola scriptura still allows consulting fallible sources; Protestants accept the creed insofar as it agrees with Scripture. | He said sola scriptura does not mean rejecting every fallible authority. Protestants may consult councils, fathers, synods, and traditions as real but subordinate authorities; they hold the Nicene Creed because they judge it to agree with Scripture, not because the council is intrinsically infallible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=9428" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:08</a> | What should someone do if YouTube keeps recommending Dan McClellan or similar content to a family member? | He said do not hit dislike; use the "not interested" option because dislike still counts as engagement. | He explained that YouTube treats dislikes as interaction and may show more of the same content when a viewer dislikes it. His practical advice was to use the three-dot menu and mark the video or channel as not interested instead. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=9664" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:41:04</a> | Is Dan McClellan right that the Luke 2 census could not have happened because Roman censuses never required travel? | He said no; censuses could require people in rural communities to go to an appropriate town, and timing could avoid harvest disruption. | He argued that censuses had different purposes and did not necessarily require impossible mass travel. In a rural setting, people could go into town or to the relevant ancestral city, and Joseph likely had transport as a carpenter; Mary's pregnancy made the trip difficult, not the census itself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=9901" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:01</a> | Does Romans 11:26-27 quoting Isaiah 59:20-21 show a translation problem or problems with Scripture in Paul's time? | He said Paul appears to be using a Septuagint form, and the issue depends on which Greek textual tradition was in play. | He compared Romans with Isaiah and said Romans 11:26 especially lines up with a Septuagint reading. Because there were multiple Septuagint versions circulating, he said the question becomes which textual form Paul had, rather than a simple claim that Scripture was broken. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=10220" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:50:20</a> | How do you answer someone who says cultural context can excuse discarding anything in the Bible? | He said that is not real contextual reading; everyone reads with some context, so the issue is whether one uses the ancient context or a modern one. | He argued that using context is not a license to erase the text's meaning. Everyone reads through some context, so the question is whether the interpreter is trying to recover the Bible's own ancient context or importing a modern context to override what the text intended. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=10699" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:19</a> | How did the KJV-only movement start? | He traced it to late nineteenth-century reactions against critical Greek texts and newer English translations, amplified by guilt-by-association arguments. | He connected the movement to reactions against newer critical texts and translations, especially when textual criticism became associated with liberal scholars. He also said language change fueled the dispute: when translators updated words whose meaning had shifted since 1611, KJV-only advocates treated that as corrupting Scripture rather than normal translation work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=11285" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:08:05</a> | How should one respond to claims that Catholicism has a lot of paganism in it? | He said that claim goes too far; Catholicism has accretions and errors, but not a lot of paganism. | He distinguished paganism from accretions, meaning extra-biblical practices that become attached to a tradition. Some practices may have origins or parallels outside Christianity, but he said they have often been thoroughly Christianized, so criticism should be more precise than simply calling them pagan. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=11931" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:18:51</a> | What are the ramifications of spiritual practices like yoga and Ouija boards? | He distinguished ordinary secular stretching from spiritually oriented yoga, warned against occult forms, and said Ouija boards should be avoided. | He distinguished secular exercise labeled yoga from religious or meditative yoga meant to engage spiritual realities, saying the latter is dangerous. He treated Ouija boards as occult practice and gave an unambiguous warning to avoid them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=12604" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:30:04</a> | How should Christians approach slavery in the Bible? | He said slavery is descriptive rather than prescriptive in the Bible, with different forms regulated and progressively restricted rather than endorsed. | He stressed that biblical slavery is a large specialist topic with multiple forms, and that the Bible describes and regulates existing practices rather than commanding people to institute slavery. He pointed to distinctions between Israelite debt servitude and war captives and argued that regulation, limits, and release provisions matter for interpreting the texts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=12893" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:34:53</a> | Is there a scientific basis for the parting of the sea in Exodus, or was it purely supernatural? | He said there is a scientific basis called wind setdown, where an east wind can temporarily part or lower water. | He described wind setdown as an observed phenomenon in which wind blowing across a body of water changes its level and can expose a crossing area. He said it is still seen in the eastern Nile Delta and has been discussed in scientific papers, making it a plausible mechanism for the Sea of Reeds. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=12955" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:35:55</a> | Is the "I am" statement in John 8:58 a direct proclamation related to Exodus 3:14? | He said yes, and argued the surrounding reaction shows the audience understood it as a major divine claim. | He connected John 8:58 to the divine "I am" language of Exodus 3:14 and said the reaction in John 8 supports that reading, since the audience picks up stones after the statement. He noted the Greek and likely underlying Semitic formulation, then summarized the claim as Jesus making a direct divine self-identification. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=13532" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:45:32</a> | What are your thoughts on the doctrine of original sin? | He said there are multiple versions, and he favors a federal-headship or representative-authority account rather than a genetic-contagion model. | He said "original sin" is not a single uniform doctrine. He accepts a version grounded in federal or representative authority, where Adam acts as a head whose action affects those under him, but he rejects a model in which sin is biologically or genetically transmitted like a contagion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=13655" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:47:35</a> | Do temple-inscription numbers show number idioms outside the Bible? | He said yes; idiomatic number use appears in several inscriptions outside the Bible. | He named examples such as the Mesha Stela, temple dedication inscriptions, and the 400-Year Stela. His point was that number idioms are not only a biblical phenomenon. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=13695" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:48:15</a> | What about claims that the Council of Rome had an early canon list including the Apocrypha? | He said those claims rest on the Gelasian Decree, which he considers a forgery, and there are no reliable records from that council. | He argued that the Gelasian Decree cannot serve as a reliable record because it quotes Augustine, who was not active at the time in question. Since no solid proceedings or secondary records from the Council of Rome survive, he rejected claims built on that decree as having a very poor foundation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=14583" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:03:03</a> | In the Joseph story, who were the Egyptian "magicians" or reader-priests, and was that a specific kind of priest? | He said they were likely lector or reader priests connected with dream interpretation, though the exact etymology is not perfectly clear. | After asking for the verse, he discussed Genesis 41:8 and 41:24 and said the Hebrew term points to interpreters of dreams rather than ordinary priests. He connected the function to Egyptian lector priests who had access to scrolls and dream manuals, while noting that the exact linguistic derivation is uncertain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=14733" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:05:33</a> | Was there backlash from the recent popcorn review of Frank Turek's Exodus-evidence video? | He said no major backlash had appeared, perhaps because the Billy Carson debate had overshadowed it. | He said the popcorn review dealt with Frank Turek's Exodus-evidence material, but he had not seen major backlash. He thought the Billy Carson debate had become the bigger YouTube story that week and had probably eclipsed the review. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CQdYUSe3hV4?t=15855" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:24:15</a> | Where did Egyptians think dreams came from, especially good, bad, or foretelling dreams? | He said they generally thought dreams came from the gods, who could send omens or knowledge of the future. | He said Egyptians thought dreams could come from the gods, who knew or could disclose the future and could send good or bad omens. He named gods associated with magic, writing, or oracles, and said many gods could send dream oracles, including good dreams or nightmares depending on their disposition toward a person. |
